headlines.title: San Jose business collects relief supplies for Venezuela earthquakes victims
headlines.description: Several businesses in the Bay Area are coming together to collect relief supplies for Venezuela following the deadly twin earthquakes that struck the nation. This includes a Venezuelan food truck in San Jose. The death toll in Venezuela is climbing as the desperate search for survivors continues following two major earthquakes. Authorities confirmed 1450 have died and nearly 69,000 are still missing. Several Bay area businesses came together to collect relief supplies, including Aquenono in San Jose. On Sunday, the husband and wife owners of the food truck business have been collecting everything, from gloves to food, to walkers and dog food. They say many of their clients have loved ones in Venezuela. Oakland 14 hours ago Venezuela 18 hours ago San Jose 20 hours ago “They can’t get in contact with family. There’s no news about their whereabouts of their family member and we hear many more stories like that from clients probably because their loved ones still have not been identified yet,” said Aquenono co-owner Melanie Portilla. Portilla says her brother-in-law lives in Venezuela and is grappling with aftershocks and power outages. So far, about 45 people have dropped off relief supplies, which will be brought to Oakland and later flown to Venezuela.

headlines.title: Volunteers start dismantling Pink Triangle on Twin Peaks in San Francisco
headlines.description: Volunteers started dismantling the Pink Triangle on Twin Peaks Sunday as San Francisco’s Pride festivities come to an end. For more than three decades now, the giant Pink Triangle on Twin Peaks has also been an expression of pride and resistance over San Francisco that can be seen from miles away during Pride Month. It took hundreds of volunteers more than three hours to tear down, and a lot longer than that to set up. Patrick Carney and a friend say they came up with this idea of a giant symbol of pride for their community more than 31 years ago over breakfast. They decided Twin Peaks was the perfect backdrop, so they painted huge canvases, sewed them together, and placed the first Pink Triangle here in the dark of night for everyone to see the next morning. 31 years later, the Pink Triangle has become this massive annual expression for the LGBTQ+ communiyt. Each side of the triangle is 240 feet long, and it takes 5,000 heavy-duty steel spikes to hold it in place in the Bay Area winds. Why the triangle? The triangle was used by the Nazi’s during World War II as a way to identify homosexuals in concentration camps. Carney says it stands as resistance to those ideas and as a symbol of hope and a lot more. “Resistance, courage, persistence, empowerment. But it started out during the holocaust, and then in the 70s it came back as a symbol of gay liberation and resistance”, he said. Volunteers stayed until 8 p.m. to take down the Pink Triangle and put it away in storage until Pride Month 2027 kicks off.

headlines.title: Volunteers needed in Oakland to help sort donations for Venezuela
headlines.description: A community in Oakland came together to collect money and donations for Venezuela following the deadly twin eartquakes that struck the country; now, they are asking for volunteers to help sort those donations. It has been four days since the deadly earthquakes that killed more than 1,400 in Venezuela. Workers at Oakland’s Arepas restaurant have been collecting money and donated supplies. The hundreds of boxes they have include food, medicine, clothing and diapers. However, organizers said that right now, the biggest need is for volunteers. Some volunteers are also needed to guard over the donations that have been arriving every day.

headlines.title: San Jose community mobilizes to support victims of Venezuela earthquakes
headlines.description: Watching the devastation in Venezuela has prompted dozens of people throughout the Bay Area to spring into action to donate relief supplies. On Saturday, there was a donation drive at Arepas Latin Cuisine, a Venezuelan restaurant with locations in San Francisco and San Jose; many who went to the San Jose location to donate said they have relatives in Venezuela, and they are heartbroken the death toll is rising. Dorly Lopez’s cousins live in Venezuela, so for her, the mission is personal. “They have to move. And some fear there is going to be a tsunami,” she said. Lopez stopped at the restaurant with full arms and a heavy heart. Venezuela Jun 25 Venezuela Jun 27 Venezuela Jun 25 “We want to help. we see the disaster in Venezuela we are heartbroken,” she said. Lopez was among many others who were dropping off sleeping pads, clothing, water, first aid kits and diapers at the restaurant. There were so many donations that employees had to stuff the items into a closet and a van. Not only is Arepas collecting supplies, but the restaurant is also donating a portion of the profits from meals served here to relief efforts in Venezuela. For Marianny Mata, watching the devastation from the quake has been especially painful. “I was born and raised in Venezuela and my family lives there,” she said. Mata worries about those close to her. “My godmother… her uncle is missing. He’s been missing and they still haven’t found him,” she said. A video from Caracas captured the moment buildings collapsed following two back-to-back magnitude seven plus quakes, prompting the Arepas restaurant in San Francisco to also collect supplies for families in need. “It made me feel horrible made me feel responsible. I just really need to do something,” restaurant owner Joanna Torres said. As the frantic search for survivors continues in Venezuela, more than four thousand miles away people in the Bay Area are answering the call to help. Medical, baby supplies and nonperishable food were top priorities. The first shipment of donations was expected to head to the Red Cross in Caracas on Tuesday.

headlines.title: Firefighters battle large fire at a home in Castro Valley
headlines.description: Firefighters were battling a large fire at a home in Castro Valley. The fire was reported just before 4:15 p.m. at a home on Queen Street. According to authorities, the flames also threatened other homes nearby, prompting a three-alarm response. This is a developing story. Details may change as more information becomes available. Stay tuned for updates.

headlines.title: Sinkhole causes small flooding in San Francisco area
headlines.description: In San Francisco, people were told to avoid the area of Bay and Gough streets after a small sinkhole popped up Sunday afternoon. The sinkhole caused some small flooding, according to a video on Citizen App. Crews were on scene working to repair the damage. NBC Bay Area reached out for an update of the repairs but hasn’t heard back.

headlines.title: 2 arrested after double stabbing in San Francisco
headlines.description: Two people were arrested following a double stabbing in San Francisco Sunday, police said. Police said officers responded to the 7th and Market streets regarding a stabbing at around 6:11 p.m. There, they found two victims suffering from stab wounds, according to authorities. The victims were taken to the hospital with non-life-threatening injuries, police said. The police department reported officers on scene found and detained two subjects. Officers developed probable cause to arrest the two subjects in connection with the stabbing, officials said. Police asked anyone with information to contact them at 415-575-4444 or text a tip to TIP411 and begin the message with SFPD.

headlines.title: Thousands celebrate Pride with 59th annual parade in San Francisco
headlines.description: In San Francisco, tens of thousands of people packed the Pride Parade route Sunday. The Pride celebration kicked off with the 56th annual Pride Parade, one of the largest and most legendary LGBTQ+ celebrations in the world. About 25,000 people participated in the 1.6-mile route from the Embarcadero to Civic Center Plaza, advocating for equality, freedom, and their culture. “This is exactly what it means. All of us being here together, always supporting our own community, the LGBT community, which I am a part of. So proud to be here and to always continue to show support”, Theo said. The theme of this year’s celebration. “Resistance In Action”, comes at a time when many say their rights are under attack. Many in the parade holding signs condemning Trump administration policies. San Francisco Mayor Daniel Lurie, State Senator Alex Padilla, and Speaker Emerita Nancy Pelosi, promised San Francisco will resist as long as there is a threat to anyone. “We don’t agonize; we organize. This year, the anniversary of our country, we will pay tribute to the LGBTQ community for being so strong in resistance. Resistance is our hope”, Pelosi said. “We know that civil rights, including LGBTQ, are under attack by this administration”, Padilla said. Senator Scott Weiner made an appearance, surrounded by his security detail after being surrounded and heckled by protestors Friday night. Pride Faithfully say they’ll always show their joy proudly. The party at Civic Center Plaza goes on until 6 p.m.

headlines.title: Violent incidents during Pride celebrations in San Francisco bring concerns
headlines.description: The San Francisco Pride weekend is in full swing, but a few violent incidents, including a shooting near an entrance in the Castro District, vandalism and two cases where state Sen. Scott Wiener said he was “harassed, threated and intimidated” brought on concerns. That violence caught the attention of millions of people on social media, on a weekend when the world’s eyes were on the Bay Area for Pride and World Cup. One of those incidents was posted on social media. The viral video showed a crowd during Friday’s Trans March shouting at congressional candidate Wiener, who eventually chased him away. After having a day to process what happened, Wiener said he felt “harassed, threatened and intimidated.” “But when folks are basically swarming around you, getting really close, and then touching you, and just trying to drive you out of the park physically, that’s not okay. That crosses a line,” he said. San Francisco Jun 27 San Francisco Jun 27 Wiener said some people got physical with him, and for the first time in 22 years, he left the march because he didn’t feel safe. “Which makes me really sad, because it’s the first Trans March I’ve ever missed since it started in 2004, but it would not have been safe for me or for my team,” he said. Wiener said his staff was also accosted in a separate incident while attending a World Cup watch party Wednesday night in the Mission District. “A guy who had previously harassed me, in a very antisemitic way, sort of cornered me, and blocked me and was getting very close to me and being very aggressive,” he said. On Friday’s, the incident that occurred during the Trans March left to San Francisco police officers injured and five people under arrest. SFPD released a statement, which in part read: “The SFPD always respects individuals First Amendment rights to protest; however, criminal activity will not be tolerated in San Francisco.” Pride participants told NBC Bay Area they were aware of what happened but are not too worried. “You can’t, and that kind of violence and threat of intimidation because if you stay away because you’re afraid something might happen they’ve already won,” San Francisco resident Sister Roma said. “I love being here. I love seeing everybody, seeing everybody happy. This is what pride is about. It’s just about the beauty of being in community and just being together with people and feeling that love,” ally Jason said. Wiener agreed with them and the SFPD. He said he respected everyone’s rights to disagree and voice their own political views, but he noted that too often, people crossed the line into harassment, intimidation and bullying. “We need to fight them, and stick up for each other, and that’s what Pride is about. It’s about celebrating the community and recommitting to the fight ahead,” he said. Wiener said he would attend the main Pride weekend event–the San Francisco Pride Parade and Celebration–which was held on Sunday morning.

headlines.title: Electric bicyclist killed in Fremont crash, police report
headlines.description: A man riding an electric bicycle was killed when he was struck by a driver near an on-ramp to Interstate 880 in Fremont on Saturday, authorities said. At around 4:30 p.m., officers responded to the area of Auto Mall Parkway and Grimmer Boulevard regarding a major traffic collision, according to the Fremont Police Department. The crash occurred while a car was driving westbound on Auto Mall Parkway in a designated right-turn lane to get onto northbound I-880. The man was riding eastbound in the westbound bike lane at the on-ramp entrance to the highway and the two collided, police said. The victim, who has not yet been identified, suffered head trauma and was taken to a hospital, where he was pronounced dead. The unidentified driver of the car, only described as a woman, was not injured. She remained at the scene and cooperated with investigators, the department stated. Saturday’s collision marked the sixth fatal crash in Fremont this year, authorities reported.

headlines.title: Person arrested after shooting near Pride festivities in San Francisco
headlines.description: A person was arrested and accused of shooting another near the Pride festivities in San Francisco Saturday, according to police. Police said at around 3:30 p.m., officers were at the United Nations Plaza are monitoring Pride events when a shooting occurred. Officers found a victim suffering from gunshot wounds and rendered aid, according to the department. Authorities said the victim was taken to a local hospital for non-life-threatening injuries. Officers in the area found a possible suspect and foot pursuit ensued, police said. Authorities said they were able to apprehend him for the investigation, and he was placed under arrest. A police officer resulted injured from the pursuit, and was taken to the hospital, according to authorities. The police department said this remains an active investigation and encourages anyone with information to contact them at 415-575-4444 or text a tip to TIP411 and begin the message with SFPD.

headlines.title: Authorities investigate fire at San Jose school
headlines.description: An investigation is underway following a fire at Blackford Elementary School in San Jose Saturday, authorities said. According to San Jose Fire, the incident was reported at around 2:25 p.m. The flames were contained to one room and knocked down at 2:40 p.m., according to authorities. No injuries were reported, and the fire department said their Arson Unit is investigating origin and cause.
